title: Effects of Spirulina Supplementation on Mental Function in Healthy Adults: a Randomized Controlled Trial

summary.hook: This randomized controlled trial tested whether spirulina supplementation (3 g/day for 8 weeks) improves mood and cognitive performance in healthy adults compared with placebo.
summary.summary_30s: This randomized controlled trial tested whether spirulina supplementation (3 g/day for 8 weeks) improves mood and cognitive performance in healthy adults compared with placebo. Mood was assessed using the POMS questionnaire (six subscales), and cognition using the Trail Making Test (TMT-A, TMT-B, and TMT B–A). Results suggest modest improvements: some mood subscales show significant pre/post time effects, Total POMS shows a trend in the spirulina group (paired t-test p=0.074), and cognitive flexibility (TMT B–A) improved with a significant 8-week change difference (p=0.025), though between-group interactions were not significant by repeated-measures ANOVA (p>0.05).
talktrack_2m: Focus: Evaluate whether spirulina supplementation improves mood and mental acuity in healthy adults compared with placebo.
Approach: Population: Healthy adults from a campus community. Primary outcome: Mood (POMS total and subscales).
Finding: Total POMS score: Spirulina group shows a trend toward improvement from baseline to week 8 (pre/post paired t-test p=0.074).

Why it matters: * Authors’ stated conclusions: Spirulina (3 g/day for 8 weeks) has modest impacts on mental function, indicated by reductions in mood states (POMS) and improved cognitive flexibility/visual attention/task switching (TMT).
key_numbers: Total POMS pre/post in spirulina group: p=0.074 (paired t-test).
Between-group interactions not significant (repeated-measures ANOVA; p>0.05).
This randomized controlled trial tested whether spirulina supplementation (3 g/day for 8 weeks) improves mood and cognitive performance in healthy adults compared with placebo.
takeaways: Total POMS score: Spirulina group shows a trend toward improvement from baseline to week 8 (pre/post paired t-test p=0.074).
POMS subscales (tension/anger/fatigue etc.) show asterisked significant time effects in some panels (exact p-values not printed).
TMT outcomes: Asterisks denote significant pre/post time effects; however between-group interactions not significant (repeated-measures ANOVA p>0.05).
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What was the sample size per group (SPIR vs CON), and were groups balanced at baseline?

what_to_ask.question: Total POMS in SPIR showed p=0.074—what effect size does that correspond to (Cohen’s d) and what was the direction/magnitude?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: Did you adjust for multiple comparisons across six POMS subscales and multiple TMT outcomes?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: For healthy adults, what is the practical implication—should spirulina be recommended for mood/cognition, or is evidence still preliminary?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Planning a larger trial powered for group×time interactions and multiple-comparison control
